text: Looking for a quick and punchy survey of Austrian history? A rundown of the basic timeline to help make sense of the more in-depth content of my Gretel Guides tours? The next 10 minutes is an overview of some of the major moments that have shaped Austria over the last 40,000 years or so. We start with the Late Stone Age, around the time early modern humans began to replace Neanderthals in Europe. A number of artifacts from this period, especially cutting implements and Venus figurines now displayed in Vienna's Natural History Museum, represent some of the world's earliest examples of figurative artwork. Starting around the 8th century BC, these prehistoric peoples began mining the rich underground salt deposits of the Alps. Experimentation with the other minerals and metals they found there led to Bronze and Iron Age innovations. Among the most advanced of these proto-Celtic cultures were the Hallstatt and Latene civilizations, which developed a reputation for producing high-quality steel. It became known as Noric steel to the Romans, who had christened the area Noricum and eventually assimilated it into the Roman Empire in the 1st century AD. Along the Danube River, which formed the border of their empire, the Romans established a series of legionary forts. One of these, called Vindabona, became the base of their Danubian naval fleet when the larger and more important garrison of Carnuntum nearby was sacked by the Sarmatians in the late 4th century AD. Ultimately, the Romans' territorial extent proved untenable as waves of Germanic tribes presented a constant onslaught to the declining Roman Empire, and the Noric and Pannonian borders were overrun in the early 5th century AD. Though no longer a Roman military base, Vindabona continued to prove a hotly contested settlement over the ensuing six centuries or so, occupied in turn by the Goths, Lombards, Avars, Heruli, Bavarians, and Magyars in the early medieval period. In 976, the first of Austria's two ruling families, the Babenbergs, assumed control of the area, which was termed the Osterichie, or Eastern Realm, from which is derived the modern German name for Austria, Österreich. In the mid-12th century, the Babenbergs moved their seat of rule to Vindabona, now known instead as Wien or Vienna, the name probably taken from one of the rivers on which it sits. The Babenbergs' roughly 300-year reign came to an end in the mid-13th century, when the male line died out. It was succeeded by a decades-long period called the Interregnum, in which several figures struggled to assume rule. But ultimately it was Rudolf of Habsburg who was elected as king of the Germans in 1273 and defeated his rival, Schemschel-Ottekar II of Bohemia, in the Battle of Marchfeld in 1278. This marked the beginning of a dynasty that would rule Austria for the next 640 years, also occupying the throne of the Holy Roman Empire nearly continuously from 1440 until its dissolution in 1806. After splitting into Spanish and Austrian branches, it was in the 17th century that the House of Habsburg reached its zenith in terms of territorial expansion, religious influence, and political control, ruling lands that extended across most of Central and Western Europe to colonies in North and South America and Southeast Asia. Much of their dynastic success can be traced to their marriage and family planning strategy. Habsburg rulers regularly had large numbers of children, married them off to other important families around Europe, waited for them to have children themselves, and then married those children to each other—in other words, first cousins marrying first cousins. While this proved a comparatively steady and conflict-free method of annexing territory, the 17th century also saw a culmination of genetic issues resulting from the generations of intermarriage. When the Spanish branch couldn't produce an heir and died out, the War of the Spanish Succession definitively severed the Iberian Peninsula, Spanish Netherlands, and Spain's overseas territories from Habsburg control. Ensuing centuries continued to whittle away Austrian hegemony. The Napoleonic Wars and the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire in the early 1800s were followed by fomenting nationalist sentiments that ultimately culminated in Europe-wide revolutions in 1848. When the Kingdom of Hungary won parliamentary autonomy, a restored constitution, and territorial sovereignty in 1867, this prompted the name change to Austria-Hungary, a dual monarchy with separate parliaments and prime ministers, but ruled jointly by Emperor Franz Josef I. Austria-Hungary was geographically the second-largest country in Europe after the Russian Empire, and it continued to be plagued by nationalist, separatist sentiments. In June of 1914, a Bosnian Serb named Gavrilo Princip assassinated the heir to the throne of Austria-Hungary, Archduke Franz Ferdinand, and his wife Sophie as they toured Sarajevo in an open car. Austria-Hungary reacted by threatening Serbia with military action. Russia intervened on Serbia's behalf, prompting Germany to come to Austria-Hungary's aid, and as France was allied with Russia and German military strategy involved attacking Belgium to get to France, Britain was compelled to come to Belgium's aid. These powers' extensive colonial holdings and naval activity also meant that hostilities spilled out across the globe. After four years of worldwide warfare, an estimated 40 million people were dead, and the four major European empires—German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian—had come to an end, making way for a number of new independent nation-states. Austria, as you see it on the map today, was what was left of the former massive Austro-Hungarian Empire, and a new government was established, the so-called First Republic, with a bicameral parliament, a chancellor, and a president. But economic insecurity, political instability, and growing support of fascism metastasized in February 1934 with the Austrian Civil War. Parliament was dissolved, Austria was declared a one-party state, elections were suspended, the chancellor was assassinated, and Adolf Hitler and the German Nazi Party were able to manipulate this morass to their political advantage, ultimately annexing Austria in March of 1938. At this point, Austria became the Ostmark, or the Eastern Territory, of the Third Reich. Nearly one million Austrians fought in the German military during the Second World War, and while Austrian territory was largely spared much of the destruction that leveled other areas in Western Europe, at least until the end of the war, Austria did play an active role in supplying the front, developing and producing weapons technology, and operating numerous concentration camps where millions were tortured and murdered. Following the end of the war in 1945, Austria, like Germany, was occupied and governed by a coalition of the victorious Allied forces, who divided the country and its capital into four zones. This occupation period ended in 1955, with the signing of the Austrian State Treaty and Declaration of Neutrality, celebrated annually on October 26th. The treaty re-established a free, sovereign, and democratic Austria, and marks the beginning of a period commonly called the Second Republic, which continues to this day, a multi-party semi-presidential federal parliamentary republic. That means that there is an executive branch, headed by a president who is elected every six years and leads a cabinet that includes the chancellor, vice-chancellor, and departmental  The executive branch under the president is largely answerable to the Austrian legislative branch of government, which consists of a bicameral parliament. One of these parliamentary chambers is the Federal Council, or Bundesrat, made up of 62 members elected by each of Austria's nine provincial legislatures for five or six years. While the Federal Council is often referred to as the Upper House, its powers are largely eclipsed by those of the other parliamentary chamber, the National Council, or Nationalrat. Its 183 members are elected every five years, and in order to govern, those members must have a mandate. This usually involves multiple parties forming a coalition in order to achieve majority in the council. The party with a mandate or a majority in the National Council is also commonly the one to put forward the chancellor, who is a similar figure to a prime minister in other parliamentary democracies. Austria also has an independent judicial branch, which, in addition to a system of courts dedicated to civil, criminal, administrative, and constitutional matters, includes the officers of the state prosecutors and corrections system. Austria has been a member of the European Union since 1995, joined the Schengen Zone in 1997, and has been on the Euro since 1999. As of 2021, it has 19 representatives in the European Parliament. It hosts a number of international and non-governmental organizations, as well as being a seat of the United Nations. The population of Austria hovers just under 9 million, according to the 2020 census, with just under 2 million of those inhabitants living in Vienna, its capital. The country's official language is German, but a number of other languages and dialects are also recognized, and English is broadly taught in schools. It consistently ranks among the top 20 richest countries by GDP per capita, and Vienna is often named among the world's most livable cities. That about brings us up to the present day. I hope this overview has given you a general understanding of some of the major milestones in Austrian history and enables you to better enjoy your experience exploring Vienna.
